source_metadata.title_raw: James H. Wilkins - A-18-227 - November 6, 2018 - Fresno

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
November 6, 2018 
James H. Wilkins 
Wilkins, Drolshagen, & Czeshinski, LLP 
6785 N. Willow A venue 
Fresno, CA 93 710 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-227 
Dear Mr. Wilkins: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act")1 and Government Code section 1090. Please note that we are 
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice 
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Tulare County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097. l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097. l(c)(5).) 
QUESTIONS 
1. Assuming Councilmember Greg Nunley has a conflict of interest under the Act in a 
Tulare City Council decision regarding an application to rezone property he solely owns, does the 
Act prohibit him from recusing himself and participating as a member of the general public in the 
decision? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Councilmember Nunley from recusing himself and 
participating as a member of the public in a City Council decision regarding an application to 
rezone property he solely owns? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-18-227 
Page No. 2 
1. No. As long as Councilmember Nunley follows proper procedures for recusal, he may 
appear before the City Council to represent his personal interest in his real property, as discussed 
below. 
2. No. As long as the rezoning decision is regulatory in nature (and not contingent on 
Councilmember Nunley providing some type of public improvement or facility as consideration), 
Section 1090 would not apply to the decision, and would not thus prohibit him from participating as 
a member of the public in the decision. 
FACTS 
You represent Councilmember Nunley who sits on the Tulare City Council. Councilmei;nber 
Nunley owns a construction and development company and one of his projects in Tulare, known as 
Oak Creek 4, needs a zoning change. The real property where the zoning change is sought, which 
requires approval by the Tulare City Council, is owned solely by Councilmember Nunley. Your 
request for advice presumes that Councilmember Nunley has a conflict of interest should he wish to 
participate in the zoning decision in his capacity as a Councilmember. He would therefore like to 
recuse himself from participation on any City Council discussions or decision regarding this matter 
in his official capacity as a Councilmember, but would like to sit in the audience at the City Council 
meeting and present the matter to the City Council as the owner of the real property when the 
matter is called. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in the 
making, or otherwise using his or her official positi_on to influence a governmental decision in 
which the official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial 
interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in 
that section. 
As mentioned, your request assumes Councilmember Nunley has a conflict of interest under 
the Act. Therefore, Regulation 18704 specifies he is prohibited from doing any of the following: 
(a) Making a Decision. A public official makes a governmental 
decision if the official authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints a 
person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or 
enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. 
(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a 
governmental decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a 
recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without 
significant intervening substantive review. 


File No. A-18-227 
Page No. 3 
(c) Using Official Position to Attempt to Influence a Decision. A 
public official uses his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision if he or she: 
(I) Contacts or appears before any official in his or her agency or in 
an agency subject to the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency 
for the purpose of affecting a decision; or 
(2) Contacts or appears before any official in any other government 
agency for the purpose of affecting a decision, and the public official acts or 
purports to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency 
in making the contact. 
(Regulation 18704, subds. (a)-(c)(2).) 
Appearance as a Member of the General Public 
Regulation 18704( d)(2), however, provides an exception that permits an otherwise 
disqualified official to appear before his or her agency in the course of its prescribed governmental 
function as a member of the general public on matters solely related to the official's personal 
interests. The exception applies to real property "owned entirely by the official, members of his or 
her immediate family, or the official and members of his or her immediate family." (Regulation 
18704(d)(2)(A).) 
Based on the facts provided, Councilmember Nunley would have a "personal interest" in the 
decision at issue based on his sole ownership of the real property he seeks to have rezoned. Thus, he 
may appear before and communicate with the City Council in his private capacity as a member of 
the general public to represent his personal interest in the real property. This exception is 
interpreted narrowly and does not allow Councilmember Nunley to provide general comments 
regarding the matter. Also, this exception would apply only in those instances where 
Councilmember Nunley makes it clear that he is not acting in an official capacity and that he is 
representing his personal interest, and his comments are limited to that personal interest. (See 
McHugh Advice Letter, No. I-98-324.) The exception does not permit Councilmember Nunley to 
represent any other person's interests or the interests of any group. 
Disqualification Procedures Under Regulation 18707 
To qualify for the exception, Councilmember Nunley must follow the procedures set forth in 
Regulation 18707. Specifically, he must publicly identify each type of financial interest involved in 
the decision that gives rise to the disqualifying conflict of interest. The identification must be made 
after the announcement of the agenda item to be discussed or voted on, but before the discussion or 
vote commences. He must also recuse himself from voting on the matter, leave the dais to speak 
from the same area as the members of the public and limit his remarks solely to his personal 
interest. Note that under Regulation 18707(a)(3)(C), Councilmember Nunley may listen to public 
discussion and deliberation of the matter with the other members of the public. 


File No. A-18-227 
Page No. 4 
Section 1090 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. The prohibition applies regardless of 
whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 
Cal.~d 633, 646.) Section 1090 applies to City officers or employees, including Councilmembers. 
An important determination in the Section 1090 analysis is whether the decision concerns a 
contract. In 89 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 258 (2006), the Attorney General stated that in determining 
whether the subject of a decision is a "contract," we look to general principles of contract law. For 
example, in the Ansolabehere Advice Letter, No. A-l 7-160a, we stated: 
While many land use decisions are 'regulatory in nature' and not 
contracts, there are some, including development agreements, that are 
contractual; involving consideration by both the local governmental entity 
and the project proponent, with the latter typically providing some type of 
public improvement or facility as a part of this consideration. In 
determining whether Section 1090 would apply in the context of land use 
decisions, we must determine whether the decision is purely regulatory, or 
whether it involves the project proponent providing a public improvement 
or similar consideration, thus making it contractual in nature.2 
Therefore, so long as the rezoning decision at issue is regulatory in nature (and not 
contingent on Councilmember Nunley providing some type of public improvement or facility as 
consideration), Section 1090 would not apply to the decision, and it would not thus prohibit him 
from appearing before and communicating with the City Council as a member of the general public 
with respect to the matter. 
JW:jgl 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
2 Note that the Attorney General's Office has stated that a development agreement is a contract for purposes of 
Section I 090 in 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230 (1995).

participating as a member of the public in a City Council decision regarding an application to 
rezone property he solely owns?

a member of the public in the decision.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Assuming Councilmember Greg Nunley has a conflict of interest under the Act in a 
Tulare City Council decision regarding an application to rezone property he solely owns, does the 
Act prohibit him from recusing himself and participating as a member of the general public in the 
decision? 
2. Does Section 1090 prohibit Councilmember Nunley from recusing himself and 
participating as a member of the public in a City Council decision regarding an application to 
rezone property he solely owns?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. As long as Councilmember Nunley follows proper procedures for recusal, he may 
appear before the City Council to represent his personal interest in his real property, as discussed 
below. 
2. No. As long as the rezoning decision is regulatory in nature (and not contingent on 
Councilmember Nunley providing some type of public improvement or facility as consideration), 
Section 1090 would not apply to the decision, and would not thus prohibit him from participating as 
a member of the public in the decision.
